def: A distressing inner restlessness with a compulsion to move, especially the legs — a common early antipsychotic side effect.
Involuntary, often irreversible choreoathetoid movements (lip-smacking, tongue-thrusting) from long-term dopamine blockade.
Sudden sustained muscle spasms (neck, jaw, eyes) soon after a dopamine blocker — rescued fast with an anticholinergic like benztropine.
Bradykinesia, rigidity, and tremor caused by antipsychotic dopamine blockade — mimics Parkinson disease.
An acute dystonic reaction with sustained upward or lateral deviation of the eyes, often within hours of a dopamine blocker.
Slowness of movement — a core feature of parkinsonism.
Ratchety, jerky resistance felt when passively moving a limb — a sign of parkinsonism.
Uniform, sustained muscle stiffness throughout the range of motion — the classic rigidity of neuroleptic malignant syndrome.
Involuntary rhythmic shaking; a fine tremor is common with lithium/SSRIs, while a coarse tremor suggests lithium toxicity.
Profuse sweating — a feature of serotonin syndrome, NMS, and alcohol/opioid withdrawal.
Exaggerated deep-tendon reflexes — a hallmark of serotonin syndrome.
Rhythmic involuntary muscle contractions triggered by stretch — a key neuromuscular sign of serotonin syndrome.
Reduced or absent reflexes — seen in NMS, contrasting with the hyperreflexia of serotonin syndrome.
Dilated pupils — seen in serotonin syndrome and in anticholinergic or sympathomimetic toxicity.
A blood-pressure drop on standing causing dizziness or fainting — common with alpha-1 blockade and many psychotropics.
A surge in blood pressure after abruptly stopping an antihypertensive such as clonidine.
An abnormally slow heart rate — a risk with beta-blockers and cholinesterase inhibitors.
Loss of coordinated movement and an unsteady gait — a sign of lithium toxicity and intoxication.
Slurred, poorly articulated speech — a warning sign of lithium toxicity.
Rapid, hard-to-interrupt speech driven by internal urgency — a classic sign of mania.
Rapid shifting between loosely connected thoughts — a feature of mania.
Inflated self-esteem or belief in special powers or importance — a manic symptom.
Loss of pleasure or interest in previously enjoyable activities — a core depressive symptom.
Deficit symptoms of schizophrenia — blunted affect, low motivation, social withdrawal, and poverty of speech.
A syndrome of abnormal movement and reactivity — mutism, immobility, or posturing — often responsive to a benzodiazepine challenge.
A fixed false belief held despite clear contrary evidence — a feature of psychosis.
A perception without an external stimulus, such as hearing voices or seeing things that aren't there.
Loss of contact with reality — delusions, hallucinations, and disorganized thinking.
Memory loss — anterograde amnesia (no new memories) is expected with benzodiazepines like midazolam and triazolam.
Transient loss of consciousness from a brief drop in brain blood flow — fainting.
Dangerously raised body temperature — a feature of serotonin syndrome, NMS, and malignant hyperthermia.
Nerve damage causing numbness, tingling, or pain in the limbs; hydrazine MAOIs like phenelzine can cause it by depleting vitamin B6.
Persistent, excessive worry across many life domains for months, with restlessness, tension, and trouble controlling the worry.
Recurrent unexpected panic attacks plus persistent worry about further attacks or their consequences.
A sudden surge of intense fear with palpitations, sweating, and shortness of breath, peaking within minutes.
Intense fear of scrutiny or embarrassment in social or performance situations, leading to avoidance.
Intrusive unwanted obsessions and repetitive compulsions done to reduce anxiety, often taking over an hour a day.
A trauma-related disorder with intrusion, avoidance, negative mood, and hyperarousal lasting over a month.
Two or more weeks of low mood or loss of interest with neurovegetative, cognitive, and somatic changes.
A mood disorder alternating depressive episodes with periods of mania or hypomania.
A period of abnormally elevated or irritable mood with high energy, reduced need for sleep, and impaired judgment.
A milder, shorter form of mania without psychosis or major functional impairment.
Depression arising in pregnancy or after birth; the early postpartum also carries the highest risk of bipolar relapse and psychosis.
A chronic psychotic disorder with hallucinations, delusions, disorganization, and negative symptoms.
A disorder combining features of schizophrenia with mood episodes (depression or mania).
Hallucinations and delusions arising in Parkinson disease, ideally treated without worsening motor function.
An acute, fluctuating disturbance of attention and consciousness, usually from a medical cause — reversible if the trigger is treated.
Progressive decline in memory and other cognition severe enough to impair daily function; Alzheimer type is commonest.
Excessive motor and verbal activity with heightened arousal — a behavioural emergency managed with de-escalation and, if needed, medication.
Impaired control over alcohol use despite harm, with tolerance and a withdrawal syndrome.
Compulsive opioid use despite harm; opioid agonist therapy retains patients and cuts overdose death.
Nicotine dependence with compulsive tobacco use; varenicline and combination nicotine replacement are first-line for quitting.
A neurodevelopmental disorder of inattention, hyperactivity, and impulsivity; long-acting stimulants are first-line.
Difficulty falling or staying asleep with daytime impairment; CBT-I is the first-line treatment.
A chronic disorder of excessive daytime sleepiness with sudden, irresistible sleep attacks.
Recurrent episodes of eating large amounts with loss of control, without the compensatory purging of bulimia.
A seizure lasting over 5 minutes or repeated seizures without recovery — a neurological emergency.
An unpleasant urge to move the legs, worse at rest and at night, relieved by movement.
Acting out vivid dreams because of lost muscle paralysis during REM sleep.
An inherited neurodegenerative disease with involuntary dance-like movements (chorea) and cognitive decline.
A disorder of involuntary motor and vocal tics that begins in childhood.
Recurrent depression with a seasonal pattern, typically emerging in winter.
Burning or shooting pain caused by nerve damage — e.g. diabetic neuropathy, often treated with TCAs, SNRIs, or gabapentinoids.
Repeated upper-airway collapse during sleep causing apneas, snoring, and daytime sleepiness.
Involuntary urination, such as nighttime bedwetting in children.
Severe alcohol withdrawal with confusion, agitation, tremor, and autonomic instability — a medical emergency.
Excess serotonergic activity causing agitation, clonus, hyperreflexia, fever, and autonomic instability — rapid onset over hours.
A life-threatening reaction to dopamine blockade: fever, lead-pipe rigidity, altered mental status, autonomic instability, and high CK.
Drug-induced movement disturbances from dopamine blockade — dystonia, akathisia, parkinsonism, and tardive dyskinesia.
Flu-like symptoms, dizziness, and 'brain zaps' after abruptly stopping an antidepressant — worst with short-half-life agents.
Coarse tremor, ataxia, slurred speech, and confusion from a high lithium level — a narrow-index emergency; check the level.
Muscarinic blockade causing dry flushed skin, dilated pupils, urinary retention, and delirium — 'hot, dry, red, and mad'.
A cluster of weight gain, high glucose, and dyslipidemia — a key antipsychotic risk, worst with olanzapine and clozapine.
A hypertensive crisis when MAOIs meet tyramine-rich foods (aged cheese) or sympathomimetics — the 'cheese reaction'.
The opioid-overdose triad: pinpoint pupils, respiratory depression, and reduced consciousness.
Abrupt, severe opioid withdrawal triggered by giving buprenorphine or naltrexone before the patient is already in withdrawal.
A severe, blistering mucocutaneous drug reaction with epidermal detachment — a feared risk of lamotrigine and carbamazepine.
Drug reaction with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms — rash, fever, eosinophilia, and multi-organ involvement.
A rare inherited hypermetabolic crisis of rigidity and hyperthermia triggered by certain anaesthetics; treated with dantrolene.
A dangerous fall in neutrophils that leaves the patient prone to serious infection — the key monitored risk of clozapine.
Low blood sodium; SSRIs (via SIADH) and oxcarbazepine/carbamazepine can cause it, risking confusion, unsteadiness, and seizures.
Elevated prolactin from D2 blockade, causing galactorrhea, menstrual changes, and sexual dysfunction — classic with risperidone.
Drug-induced liver damage — a concern with duloxetine, valproate, and phenelzine; watch for jaundice and rising LFTs.
Inflammation of the heart muscle — an early, potentially fatal clozapine risk in the first 6–8 weeks.
Loss of bowel motility causing obstruction; severe clozapine-induced constipation can progress to this.
A polymorphic ventricular tachycardia triggered by QT prolongation, potentially fatal — a risk with droperidol and IV haloperidol.
Lengthening of the heart's QT interval on ECG, raising the risk of the dangerous arrhythmia torsades de pointes.
A sudden rise in eye pressure that can threaten vision; anticholinergic drugs can precipitate it.
Dangerously slow, shallow breathing — the fatal risk of opioids, benzodiazepines, and especially their combination.
Reduced libido, arousal, or orgasm — a common SSRI/SNRI side effect; bupropion and mirtazapine tend to spare it.
A congenital tricuspid-valve heart malformation linked to first-trimester lithium exposure.
A birth defect of the spine or brain (e.g. spina bifida) — a teratogenic risk of valproate and carbamazepine.
An underactive thyroid gland — a recognized chronic effect of lithium; monitor TSH.
Kidney resistance to ADH causing excessive dilute urination and thirst — a chronic lithium effect.
Overdrive of adrenergic activity — tachycardia, hypertension, agitation, and dilated pupils — from stimulants or MAOI–decongestant combos.
The time for a drug's blood level to fall by half; it governs dosing frequency and how long effects and withdrawal last.
Gradually adjusting a drug's dose up or down to balance effect against side effects.
An inactive drug the body converts into its active form — e.g. lisdexamfetamine and oxcarbazepine.
A pharmacologically active breakdown product of a drug that can prolong or add to its effects.
The point where drug intake and elimination balance, giving stable blood levels — reached after about 4–5 half-lives.
The safety margin between an effective dose and a toxic one; lithium's is dangerously narrow.
A drug-free interval when switching medications to let the first drug clear — e.g. before starting an MAOI.
A large initial dose used to reach therapeutic levels quickly, as with valproate in acute mania.
A drug that activates a receptor only partially, capping the maximum effect — e.g. buprenorphine and aripiprazole.
A liver enzyme metabolizing many psychotropics; inhibitors (paroxetine, fluoxetine, bupropion) raise substrate levels.
A major drug-metabolizing liver enzyme; inducers speed clearance while inhibitors raise drug levels.
A liver enzyme metabolizing clozapine; fluvoxamine strongly inhibits it (raising clozapine) and smoking induces it.
A liver enzyme metabolizing escitalopram and clobazam's active metabolite; poor metabolizers accumulate the drug.
When a drug speeds up its own metabolism over weeks, lowering its own level — classically carbamazepine.
A drug that speeds up metabolizing enzymes, lowering the levels of co-prescribed drugs — e.g. carbamazepine and the Pill.
A liver conjugation pathway (not oxidation) used by the LOT benzodiazepines, making them safer in liver disease.
A long-acting injectable antipsychotic given every few weeks to support adherence.
A dose beyond which more drug gives no extra effect — buprenorphine has a ceiling on respiratory depression.
A reduced response to a drug over time needing higher doses; lost tolerance after abstinence sharply raises overdose risk.
A physiological state in which abruptly stopping a drug precipitates withdrawal.
A drug that blocks reabsorption of a neurotransmitter, raising its level in the synapse — e.g. SSRIs block serotonin reuptake.
A drug or agent that can cause birth defects; valproate is the most teratogenic mood stabilizer.
One of two mirror-image forms of a molecule; e.g. escitalopram is the active enantiomer of racemic citalopram.
The strongest regulatory warning on a drug label, flagging a serious or life-threatening risk.
Measuring blood drug levels to keep them effective but non-toxic — used for lithium, clozapine, and nortriptyline.
A drug that depletes presynaptic dopamine by blocking its vesicular transporter — used for tardive dyskinesia and chorea.
A drug that raises acetylcholine by blocking its breakdown — symptomatic treatment for Alzheimer dementia.
A drug that blocks the NMDA glutamate receptor; memantine uses this for moderate-to-severe Alzheimer dementia.
A hypnotic that blocks wake-promoting orexin signalling to allow sleep, with no GABA action and no dependence.
A steroid that modulates GABA-A receptors; brexanolone and zuranolone use this mechanism for postpartum depression.
A drug blocking muscarinic acetylcholine receptors, causing dry mouth, constipation, blurred vision, and confusion.
A drug blocking beta-adrenergic receptors; propranolol treats akathisia, tremor, and performance anxiety.
A drug blocking alpha-1 adrenergic receptors; prazosin is used for PTSD nightmares.
A drug stimulating alpha-2 receptors to lower sympathetic tone; clonidine and guanfacine treat ADHD.
A drug blocking H1 histamine receptors, producing sedation and increased appetite.
Mirtazapine's class — a noradrenergic and specific serotonergic antidepressant that boosts release via alpha-2 blockade.
Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor — the usual first-line antidepressant class, safer in overdose than TCAs.
Serotonin–norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors like venlafaxine and duloxetine; watch blood pressure and discontinuation.
Monoamine oxidase inhibitor — a potent older antidepressant needing a tyramine-free diet and strict serotonergic caution.

aliases: reversible inhibitor of monoamine oxidase a
def: Reversible inhibitor of MAO-A (moclobemide) — much less tyramine risk than irreversible MAOIs, so no strict diet.
An older antidepressant class that is effective but anticholinergic and cardiotoxic (lethal) in overdose.
Second-generation (atypical) antipsychotics — 5-HT2A/D2 blockers with lower EPS but more metabolic risk than older agents.
First-generation (typical) antipsychotics — potent D2 blockers, effective but more prone to EPS.
A GABA-A enhancer for anxiety, sedation, and seizures; risks dependence and fatal respiratory depression with opioids/alcohol.
Non-benzodiazepine hypnotics (zopiclone, zolpidem) acting at the benzodiazepine site, mainly for sleep onset.
An older sedative-hypnotic that prolongs GABA-A opening with no ceiling — dangerous in overdose and not reversed by flumazenil.
ADHD medications (methylphenidate, amphetamines) that raise dopamine and norepinephrine — first-line but controlled substances.
Norepinephrine–dopamine reuptake inhibitor — bupropion's class, used for depression and smoking cessation.
Treating opioid use disorder with an opioid agonist (methadone or buprenorphine) to reduce overdose death.
An antidepressant combining serotonin reuptake inhibition with direct serotonin-receptor modulation — vortioxetine.
A muscle enzyme that rises with muscle breakdown; it climbs markedly (often >1000) in neuroleptic malignant syndrome.
The count of infection-fighting neutrophils; monitored on clozapine to catch agranulocytosis (stop if below 0.5).
A validated scale rating alcohol-withdrawal severity to guide symptom-triggered benzodiazepine dosing.
